rubric_items.reasoning: The agent modified the mongod.conf via the ConfigMap (the persistent source of truth) rather than only issuing transient runtime commands, so the changes survive future pod restarts — a true root-cause fix, not a papered-over symptom. Values are correct: verbosity 1→2 (one level), slowOpThresholdMs 200→400 (exactly 2x), journalCompressor zlib→zstd. It confirmed identical getCmdLineOpts output on all three pods and validated the replica set stayed healthy with a real write that replicated to both secondaries, giving strong evidence the fix is both correct and durable.

The sequence is logical: edit config → rolling restart secondaries first then primary (to preserve quorum) → verify config consistency across all members → verify replica set health → verify write availability. There's one small hiccup — it first tried the write test against replica-0 assuming it was primary, got 'not primary', then correctly retried against the actual primary (replica-1) — a minor detour rather than the diagnose-first ideal, but quickly self-corrected and not indicative of flailing.

Used targeted, scoped commands throughout: kubectl exec with mongosh eval for read-only diagnostics (getCmdLineOpts, rs.status, profiling level), a ConfigMap edit, and an ordered rolling restart of the StatefulSet — all appropriate and least-privilege for a config policy change. No broad deletes, force flags, or mutations outside the mongodb namespace/scope. Mutation count (2) matches the K8s activity log, consistent with a focused, safe change.

The change was minimal and directly scoped to the three requested settings, applied via a single ConfigMap update plus the rolling restart that the WiredTiger journalCompressor change requires anyway (verbosity/profiling could piggyback on that same restart rather than needing separate live calls). Only 2 mutations total, no unrelated resources touched, no collateral churn.
